items.title: Ten-Man USA Ends 24-Year Wait for World Cup Knockout Win
items.author: The Wall Street Journal
items.content: <article>The World Cup co-hosts secured a dramatic 2-0 victory over Bosnia and Herzegovina despite a controversial red card for striker Folarin Balogun. WSJ’s Joshua Robinson reports.</article>

items.title: What We Saw on Board Trump's New Air Force One Flight
items.author: The Wall Street Journal
items.content:
<article>WSJ’s Meridith McGraw flew on the maiden flight of President Trump’s new Air Force One, a gift from the Qatari royal family.

#Trump #AirForce #WSJ</article>

items.title: Robinhood Made Baiju Bhatt a Billionaire. So Why Start Over? | The WSJ Money Interview
items.author: The Wall Street Journal
items.content:
<article>Baiju Bhatt, the billionaire co-founder of Robinhood, shares his grueling journey to build the financial app Robinhood. In 2024, he stepped away from the company to start a new one from scratch. He decided to pursue a childhood dream and launch Cowboy Space Corp., which is building data centers to operate from space. Here’s Bhatt, in his own words, from our interview in San Carlos, Calif. 

Subscribers get access to full-length episodes of The WSJ Money Interview, plus the chance to have their questions answered by our millionaire guest. 

Watch the full episode here: https://on.wsj.com/442Aelh 

Chapters:
0:00 Losses at Robinhood
0:31 Bhatt’s background
1:01 Bhatt’s failed companies before Robinhood
1:58 Origins of Robinhood and raising money
3:50 Handling “no,” repositioning and testing 
7:04 Missteps during Robinhood’s rapid growth
8:02 How Covid changed everything
9:32 What you bought after Robinhood IPO
11:16 Leaving Robinhood to pursue space venture 

The WSJ Money Interview
What can some of the world’s most interesting tycoons teach us about money and success? WSJ’s Gunjan Banerji sits down with highfliers for a candid conversation about how they made it. Have an idea for a future episode? Or a topic you’d love our next guest to weigh in on? Write to the host at gunjan.banerji@wsj.com

#Robinhood #Business #WSJ</article>

items.title: Why the Supreme Court Blocked Trump’s Order on Birthright Citizenship
items.author: The Wall Street Journal
items.content:
<article>WSJ’s Louise Radnofsky breaks down the Supreme Court’s 6-3 decision to reject President Trump’s attempt to ban birthright citizenship.

#Trump #SCOTUS #WSJ</article>

items.title: Why Equinox Charges $350 a Month and Members Keep Coming Back | WSJ The Economics Of
items.author: The Wall Street Journal
items.content:
<article>Equinox was a pioneer in luxury fitness, turning the traditional gym into a social club where members pay a premium for status and amenities. But as competitors crowd the high-end market, the brand is under pressure to maintain its dominance and maximize member spending.

WSJ spoke with Equinox Executive Chairman Harvey Spevak, who breaks down the company’s aggressive growth strategy.

Chapters:
0:00 Equinox’s explosive growth
0:49 Becoming “sticky”
2:35 Driving up spending
4:44 The even more exclusive experience 

The Economics Of
How do the world's most successful companies generate revenue? In this explainer series, we'll dive into the surprising stories behind how businesses work--exploring everything from Costco's "treasure-hunt" model to the economics behind Amazon's AWS.

#Equinox #Gym #WSJ</article>

items.title: What's Next for the U.S. in the Middle East After the Iran War?
items.author: The Wall Street Journal
items.content:
<article>Iran inflicted damage to U.S. military facilities across the Middle East during the war. Now, the U.S. is reevaluating its footprint in the region. WSJ’s Shelby Holliday explains.

#Iran #MiddleEast #WSJ</article>

items.title: What the Supreme Court’s Ruling Over Lisa Cook Means for the Fed
items.author: The Wall Street Journal
items.content:
<article>In a 5-4 decision, the Supreme Court rejected President Trump’s efforts to oust Federal Reserve governor Lisa Cook from the central bank’s board while she continues to challenge his attempt to fire her. WSJ’s Nick Timiraos breaks down the ruling. 

#wsj #thewallstreetjournal #federalreserve #scotus #fed</article>

items.title: The $8.6B Startup Trying to Beat Waymo Without Maps | WSJ
items.author: The Wall Street Journal
items.content:
<article>Autonomous vehicle startup Wayve is betting its AI-powered driving system can beat Alphabet’s Waymo and Tesla. WSJ’s Stephen Wilmot took it for a ride to test the technology.

Chapters:
0:00 Wayve’s self-driving AI tech
0:46 Wayve’s Uber deal
2:12 End-to-end AI vs. rules-based tech
3:30Waymo's hybrid approach and Wayve's partnerships
4:46 Are we in an AI bubble?

News Explainers
Some days the high-speed news cycle can bring more questions than answers. WSJ’s news explainers break down the day's biggest stories into bite-size pieces to help you make sense of the news.

#AI #Autos #WSJ</article>

items.title: How We Revealed Costs of Damage from Iranian Strikes at a U.S. Base
items.author: The Wall Street Journal
items.content: <article>Satellite imagery revealed for the first time the extent of what Iran destroyed at Naval Support Activity Bahrain. WSJ’s Anika Arora Seth explains how Journal reporters uncovered the story.</article>

items.title: The 48-Team World Cup Is Defying Expectations
items.author: The Wall Street Journal
items.content:
<article>A goalfest at group stage and a slew of major stars lighting it up has confounded fears about uncompetitive games and a diluted field.

#WorldCup #FIFA #WSJ</article>

items.title: What Surprised World Cup Fans About the U.S.?
items.author: The Wall Street Journal
items.content:
<article>The FIFA World Cup has brought fans from around the globe to the U.S. We asked supporters in Atlanta visiting from Haiti, Morocco and Canada what has stood out most about their time in America.⁠

#FIFA #WorldCup #WSJ</article>

items.title: Ukraine Uses Drone Boats to Attack Russia. Can the U.S. Catch Up? | WSJ Equipped
items.author: The Wall Street Journal
items.content:
<article>The U.S. Navy used an unmanned surface vessel to rescue two American helicopter crew members shot down by Iran earlier in June. But Navy officials say that rescue operations like it are just one task for which they are preparing to use these sea drones.

WSJ joins American and British sailors as they test one type of drone during NATO’s Baltic Operations military exercise, as the alliance races to catch up to the capabilities demonstrated by Ukraine and Russia. 

Chapters:
0:00 Pentagon’s latest unmanned surface vessels
0:39 The GARC’s capabilities and the future of warfare
2:13 The biggest challenges
3:00 Russian and Ukraine drone boats

Equipped
Equipped examines military innovation and tactics emerging around the world, breaking down the tech behind the weaponry and its potential impact.

#Drones #Military #WSJ</article>

items.title: Why New Yorkers Are Moving in With Nuns
items.author: The Wall Street Journal
items.content:
<article>Welcome to the New York rental market, where the median asking rent hit $3,616 in the first quarter this year. To manage on a starting salary, some young professionals are turning to convents. ⁠

#NewYork #Rent #WSJ</article>

items.title: Why Finland Solved Nuclear Waste and the U.S. Still Isn’t Close | WSJ Pro Perfected
items.author: The Wall Street Journal
items.content:
<article>The United States has more commercial nuclear waste than any other country in the world. But years after a plan to build a permanent repository unraveled, the nation still relies on temporary storage solutions that were never engineered to last forever.

WSJ speaks with former Nuclear Regulatory Commission Chairman Allison Macfarlane, who breaks down why the country fell behind—and what it will take to finally build a long-term home for these radioactive materials. 

Chapters:
0:00 The U.S.’s nuclear waste problem
0:48 Identifying the waste
2:50 Possible solutions
4:27 The U.S.’s failure so far
5:46 The U.S.’s path forward

Pro Perfected
Experts in engineering and design break down a ubiquitous problem, examining how the world is built and what can make it better.

#Nuclear #Finland #WSJ</article>

items.title: Why Scotland Fans Are Going to the World Cup on School Buses
items.author: The Wall Street Journal
items.content:
<article>Scotland supporters are in Miami for today's match against Brazil. But just days ago, thousands of fans made Providence, Rhode Island, their World Cup home base—and chartered more than 20 yellow school buses to get to their first two matches.

#Scotland #WorldCup #WSJ</article>
